title: Reservoir computing by thin film embedded with magnetic impurities
authors: Shuto Kamakura, Tomi Ohtsuki, Jun-ichiro Ohe
abstract: The reservoir computing based on the thin film embedded with magnetic impurities in the presence of the long-range (the dipole-dipole) interaction is numerically investigated. We simulated the magnetization dynamics by taking into account the dipole-dipole interaction and performed the handwritten-digit recognition task. Although the training data is prepared by taking spatial average in the sample, the high classification accuracy is achieved. Our result demonstrates that the long range interaction effectively encodes the complex spatial input pattern into the time domain, even when only a spatially averaged output is accessible. The proposed system paves the way for easily realizable magnetic reservoir computing.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a thin film with magnetic impurities can function as a reservoir computer for image recognition. By leveraging long-range dipole-dipole interactions, the system converts spatial patterns into temporal signals, offering a potential path toward low-cost, easy-to-fabricate spintronic machine learning hardware.
main_problem: Developing a practical, easily realizable magnetic reservoir computing system that overcomes the high power consumption and fabrication difficulties of existing spintronic hardware.
main_result: The long-range dipole-dipole interaction effectively encodes spatial input patterns into temporal magnetization fluctuations, achieving up to 75% accuracy in MNIST digit recognition using spatially averaged outputs.
method: Numerical simulations of magnetization dynamics using the OOMMF framework and the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, combined with training a linear output layer via Stochastic Gradient Descent.
model_or_system: A thin film (1um x 1um x 0.1um) embedded with randomly distributed magnetic impurities (Ni or YIG) subject to external magnetic fields, uniaxial anisotropy, exchange interaction, and long-range dipole-dipole interaction.
key_observables: Spatially averaged magnetization components and their time derivatives.
important_parameters: Impurity concentration (1% to 100%), saturation magnetization (Ms), and external magnetic field strength.
main_assumptions: The input data is prepared by taking a spatial average in the sample; the system relies on the presence of long-range dipole-dipole interactions for complex dynamics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the thin film with magnetic impurities; Figure 4 compares classification accuracy using raw magnetization versus its time derivative for Ni impurities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnetic reservoir computing, describes the physical model of a thin film with impurities, details the simulation methodology and MNIST task, presents results regarding the role of dipole interactions and material properties, and concludes with the implications for hardware implementation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (color) Schematic of the thin ﬁlm with magnetic im- purities represented by red dots. The applied magnetic ﬁeld has a spatial dependence of the input hand-writing number. The gray scale data of the image is converted into the x com- ponent of the external ﬁeld.

FIG. 2. Classiﬁcation accuracy for two magnetic materials (Ni and YIG) that have diﬀerent saturation magnetization. For the Ni impurities, results of 50% impurities system with and w/o the dipole-dipole interaction are shown.
FIG. 3. Classiﬁcation accuracy for several impurities concen- tration (Ni).
FIG. 4. Classiﬁcation accuracy for the Ni impurities (50%). Results of the magnetization component data and its time derivative data.
